name: City Rail Link (CRL)
org: City Rail Link Ltd / Auckland Council / Crown
riskRationale: Under construction, fully funded, bipartisan support. Cost overruns acknowledged but project will complete.
desc: 3.45km twin-bore rail tunnel connecting Britomart to Mt Eden, with new stations at Aotea (Queen St) and Karangahape. Creates a continuous underground loop. Opening phased 2025–2026. Cost has grown from $2.5B (2013) to $5.45B+.

name: Auckland Light Rail — Cancelled
riskRationale: Cancelled by National-led government in late 2023. Would require Labour/Green government to reinstate.
desc: Proposed light rail from Auckland CBD south through Dominion Rd to Airport and northwest to Kumeu/Huapai. Two competing designs reached procurement stage before National cancelled in December 2023 after coming to power.

name: Eastern Busway — Pakuranga to Botany
riskRationale: Under construction with committed funding. Roads-focused National government supports busways.
desc: Dedicated bus rapid transit corridor from Pakuranga to Botany, via Highland Park. First stage (Pakuranga to Highland Park) under construction. Includes pedestrian/cycle paths. Transforms travel times across eastern Auckland.

name: Penlink — Whangaparāoa Peninsula
riskRationale: Toll road under construction, backed by current government's roads priority.
desc: New 7km road linking Whangaparāoa Peninsula to SH1 at Orewa. Toll-funded. Will significantly reduce peak congestion for the 100,000+ residents of the peninsula. Under construction, due to open 2027.

name: Mill Road Corridor — Auckland
riskRationale: Roads of National Significance priority under current government. Labour opposed previous incarnations. Would be reviewed under government change.
desc: Multi-lane arterial from Manukau to Drury serving rapidly growing south Auckland. Part of the Roads of National Significance (RoNS) programme reinstated by National in 2023. Business case complete, funding negotiations ongoing.

name: Waikato Expressway — Huntly to Longswamp
riskRationale: Near completion, bipartisan support, critical freight corridor.
desc: Final stages of the Waikato Expressway — a four-lane divided highway completing the Hamilton to Auckland expressway. Remaining sections under construction around Huntly. Bipartisan support as critical freight route.

name: Tauranga Northern Link — SH2 to SH29
riskRationale: Under construction with committed funding. Road of National Significance.
desc: 12km four-lane expressway linking SH2 at Te Puna to SH29 at Tauriko. Bypasses congested Tauranga city routes for freight from Port of Tauranga. Under construction, due to open 2027.

name: Let's Get Wellington Moving — Replacement Package
org: Waka Kotahi / Wellington City Council / Greater Wellington RC
riskRationale: LGWM was cancelled by National in late 2023. Replacement package is at planning stage with reduced scope. Highly contested — any government change would alter this package.
desc: National cancelled the $6.4B Let's Get Wellington Moving programme in 2023 and is developing a replacement package focused on roads, bus network improvements, and a scaled-down rapid transit option. Wellington's transport future remains deeply contested politically.

name: New Dunedin Hospital
org: Health New Zealand / Te Whatu Ora
riskRationale: Replacement hospital under construction but with major cost overruns and design controversies. A government change may alter scope but would not cancel a partly-built hospital.
desc: New ~$3.1B teaching hospital replacing the ageing Dunedin Hospital. Cost has grown from $1.4B (2019 estimate) to $3.1B+. Inpatient tower under construction. Outpatient building design still being revised. Significant controversy over cost blowouts.

name: Local Water Done Well — National Programme
riskRationale: Replaces Labour's Three Waters / Affordable Water Reforms. National's framework would not be continued under Labour, who would likely re-establish a different central entity.
desc: National's replacement for Labour's Three Waters reforms. Framework allows councils to opt into water services entities. Estimated NZ-wide water infrastructure deficit of $120B–$185B. Critical infrastructure for water safety, wastewater, and stormwater. Policy framework is contested.

name: Wellington Water Infrastructure Renewals
riskRationale: Critical aging infrastructure replacement. Bipartisan support for water safety.
desc: Multi-year programme replacing Wellington's aging pipe network, including major trunk mains. Wellington suffers chronic water loss (~30% of supply) from leaking pipes. Programme running 2022–2030+. Critical for water resilience after 2022 January weather events.

name: Kāinga Ora Housing Development — Auckland
riskRationale: National has cut Kāinga Ora's programme and put it under a Ministerial review. Major scale-back underway. A Labour/Green government would restore funding.
desc: Kāinga Ora's Auckland housing programme — thousands of new social and affordable homes across multiple Auckland sites. National placed Kāinga Ora under a Crown Review in 2024, announcing $900M of cost cuts and a scaled-back programme. A government change would likely reverse cuts.

name: Lake Onslow Pumped Hydro — Cancelled
riskRationale: Cancelled by National government in early 2024. Would require Labour to reinstate. Enormous capex makes reinstatement uncertain even for Labour.
desc: Proposed 5GW pumped hydro storage scheme at Lake Onslow, Central Otago — New Zealand's largest ever infrastructure project. Would have provided dry-year energy security to prevent power crises. National cancelled the $16B project in January 2024, citing costs and timeline.

name: Whangārei Hospital Redevelopment
org: Health New Zealand / Te Whatu Ora
riskRationale: Business case approved but Health NZ under financial pressure from National's restructure. Timeline may slip.
desc: Full redevelopment of Northland's main hospital including new clinical buildings, emergency department, and specialist services. Northland has NZ's highest health deprivation rates. Business case approved 2023; construction timeline uncertain amid Health NZ restructure.

name: Christchurch Northern Corridor — Extension
riskRationale: Road project aligned with current government's RoNS priorities but not yet funded.
desc: Extension of the Northern Motorway (SH1) from Cranford St to Groynes Drive, reducing congestion on the northern corridor into Christchurch city. Part of the NZTA's State Highway programme. Planning stage.

name: Transpower Grid Upgrades — National
riskRationale: Regulated transmission upgrade — funded through electricity lines charges regardless of government. Bipartisan support.
desc: Transpower's 2024–2034 capital programme upgrading the national grid for electrification, renewable energy connection, and reliability. Key projects include the North Island Grid Upgrade, new substations, and HVDC pole 3 refurbishment. Funded via regulated asset base.

name: Auckland Watercare — Water Supply Upgrades
riskRationale: CCO-funded water supply infrastructure — largely insulated from government policy change.
desc: Watercare's 10-year capital programme including new water treatment capacity, trunk main replacements, wastewater upgrades, and the Huia WTP upgrade. Funded through water rates. Auckland faces water supply constraints as population grows to 2M+.

name: Defence Estate Modernisation
org: NZ Defence Force / Ministry of Defence
riskRationale: Bipartisan support for defence following 2024 Defence Policy Review. AUKUS-adjacent investments may be scaled differently but core estate modernisation would continue.
desc: Multi-year programme modernising NZDF bases at Ohakea, Burnham, Linton, Devonport, and Whenuapai. Includes new facilities, accommodation, and infrastructure. 2024 Defence Policy Review identified $12B+ investment need. Budget allocated ~$3.5B over 10 years.

name: Christchurch Metro Sport Facility
riskRationale: Under construction, bipartisan, community facility.
desc: New indoor multi-sport facility at Orangetheory Stadium precinct, Christchurch. Includes 50m swimming pool, courts, gym, and event spaces. Part of Christchurch's post-earthquake recovery. Construction underway.

name: Nelson Tasman Regional Water
org: Nelson City Council / Tasman District Council
riskRationale: Depends on Local Water Done Well framework. Contested between National and Labour policy approaches.
desc: Regional water supply upgrades for Nelson and Tasman following severe water restrictions in 2021–2022. Options include new storage dams, water treatment plants, and interconnections between systems. No final decision on scope or funding.

name: Tauranga City Centre Refresh
riskRationale: Community infrastructure under Commissioner-led council, near completion.
desc: Tauranga City Centre refresh including new Te Manawataki o Te Papa civic precinct, library, art gallery, and public spaces. Crown-commissioned council delivered programme. Multiple buildings under construction 2023–2026.

name: Queenstown Lakes Transport — Programme
riskRationale: Tourism and growth-driven transport. Active modes component likely under review under current government.
desc: Long-term transport programme for Queenstown Lakes addressing chronic congestion from tourism and rapid growth. Includes potential rapid transit, road upgrades on Frankton corridor, and active modes. No business case approved; depends on government transport priorities.

name: Hawke's Bay Cyclone Recovery — Infrastructure
org: Crown / Hawke's Bay Regional Council / Napier CC / Hastings DC
riskRationale: Emergency/resilience rebuild after Cyclone Gabrielle. Bipartisan support.
desc: Crown-funded infrastructure rebuild following Cyclone Gabrielle (February 2023) — worst natural disaster in NZ's modern history. Includes stop bank repairs, road reconstruction, electricity network rebuilding, and community facility restoration across Hawke's Bay and Tairāwhiti.

name: Hamilton Bypass — Expressway Extension
riskRationale: Road of National Significance under current government. Planning stage.
desc: New expressway sections around Hamilton to complete the Ring Road and reduce freight through Hamilton city. Part of the reinstated Roads of National Significance programme. Business case in development.

name: Ōtaki to North Wellington — Highway
riskRationale: Road project; under construction but future stages depend on government. Labour preferred rail upgrade alternative.
desc: Multi-stage upgrade of SH1 from Ōtaki to north of Wellington, improving the main arterial between Auckland and Wellington. First sections under construction. National has prioritised road over the rail upgrades Labour favoured for this corridor.

name: Pūhoi to Warkworth — Motorway Extension
riskRationale: Near completion, bipartisan support as key SH1 improvement.
desc: Extension of the Northern Motorway from Pūhoi to Warkworth — 18.5km of new four-lane motorway through the Pūhoi Valley. Due to open 2026. Continues the Auckland–Whangārei expressway towards completion.

name: Palmerston North Hospital — Rebuild
riskRationale: In the business case stage. Health NZ under financial constraint from National restructure — timing uncertain.
desc: Full rebuild of Palmerston North Hospital serving Manawatū-Whanganui region. Hospital buildings are aging and seismically challenged. Business case development underway. No construction date set. Health NZ has prioritised Dunedin and Whangārei ahead of Palmerston North.

name: Christchurch Metro Sport — Velodrome
riskRationale: Local council funded, small scale.
desc: Proposed national velodrome for Christchurch as part of the South Island city's sporting precinct. Business case endorsed by council. Funding mix of council and crown sport being negotiated.

name: Wellington International Airport Expansion
riskRationale: Privately led, commercially driven expansion. Not directly affected by government change.
desc: Proposed runway extension and terminal expansion at Wellington Airport. Runway extension (300m) would allow larger widebody aircraft and direct long-haul routes. Resource consent applied for. Airport company funded — not Crown.

name: KiwiRail — Rail Network Upgrades
riskRationale: National cut KiwiRail's Resilience and Decarbonisation fund. Ongoing uncertainty about long-term rail funding.
desc: KiwiRail's capital programme including network resilience upgrades, new locomotives, and freight terminal improvements. National cancelled the $1.4B Rail Resilience and Decarbonisation Fund (RRDF) in 2023, replacing with reduced Crown funding through NLTF. Rail funding trajectory uncertain.

name: Tairāwhiti / Gisborne Cyclone Recovery
riskRationale: Emergency resilience rebuild. Bipartisan.
desc: Infrastructure rebuild in Tairāwhiti following Cyclone Gabrielle and earlier weather events. Includes SH2 and SH35 repairs, flood protection works, and community rebuilding. Some areas face managed retreat decisions.

name: South Island Rail — Picton to Christchurch
riskRationale: Labour policy; National has not prioritised South Island rail investment.
desc: Long-term upgrade of the Coastal Pacific corridor (Picton–Christchurch) for both freight and passenger services. Would require major investment in track, tunnels, and bridges. No business case; planning study stage only.

name: Otago Corrections — Remand Prison
riskRationale: Bipartisan support for corrections capacity expansion.
desc: New remand prison facility in Otago to reduce transfer of prisoners to Christchurch or Wellington. Site selection underway. National's Corrections programme includes capacity expansion.

name: Northland Transport — RoNS Corridor
riskRationale: Four-lane expressway to Whangārei is National's Roads of National Significance priority — would be cancelled or reduced under Labour/Green.
desc: National's Roads of National Significance project to upgrade SH1 between Auckland and Whangārei to expressway standard. Includes multiple sections totalling ~100km. Business case in development. Labour opposed this scope in favour of rail and public transport.

name: University of Auckland — Campus Development
riskRationale: University-funded capital programme. Not directly government dependent.
desc: University of Auckland capital programme 2024–2030 including the Te Wānanga/Learning Commons building, engineering precinct renewal, and residential college expansion. Self-funded through university reserves and borrowing.

name: Ministry of Education — School Property Programme
riskRationale: School property investment bipartisan in principle but National has cut the programme budget. Specific projects may be deferred.
desc: MoE's ongoing school property programme: seismic strengthening, new school builds in growth areas (especially Auckland, Hamilton, Tauranga), and maintenance. National cut the school property budget by $1.4B in 2024, deferring some new school builds.

name: Interislander — New Cook Strait Ferries
riskRationale: National cancelled the previous ferry+port contract ($551M cancellation fee) and is procuring differently. Still highly uncertain.
desc: Replacement of Interislander's aging Cook Strait ferry fleet and associated Wellington and Picton port infrastructure. Previous $3.1B contract cancelled by National in 2023 ($551M cancellation fee paid). New procurement underway with different scope and approach.

name: Ōpōtiki Harbour Development
org: Ōpōtiki District Council / Bay of Plenty RC
riskRationale: Small regional project, largely complete.
desc: Development of Ōpōtiki's harbour to enable commercial aquaculture vessels, supporting the Ōhiwa Harbour marine farming precinct. Crown-funded as regional economic development. Near completion.

name: Napier Port — Wharf 6 Extension
riskRationale: Commercially funded port infrastructure.
desc: New Wharf 6 extension at Napier Port to increase container handling capacity. Commercially funded by Napier Port (part-listed company). Serves Hawke's Bay horticultural export sector.

name: Ara Tūhono — Pūhoi to Wellsford
riskRationale: Extension of RoNS to Wellsford — major road investment under National. Would be cancelled under Labour/Green.
desc: Four-lane expressway extension from Warkworth to Wellsford, continuing the Northern Motorway to reduce travel times to Northland. National's RoNS programme. Highly contested — Labour opposed; Greens oppose. No consent, no funding — decades away.

name: Tū Tama Wāhine — Māori Housing Auckland
riskRationale: Māori housing programmes under pressure from Kāinga Ora cuts.
desc: Māori-led affordable housing developments across south and west Auckland in partnership with Kāinga Ora and iwi. Multiple developments under construction. Programme at risk from Kāinga Ora budget cuts announced in 2024.

name: Bay of Plenty Expressway — Tauranga to Rotorua
riskRationale: National's RoNS agenda; not yet funded or consented.
desc: Proposed expressway upgrade between Tauranga and Rotorua to replace the existing winding SH33 and SH30 routes. Early planning stage under National's Roads of National Significance programme.

name: Wellington Harbour Quays — Seismic Strengthening
riskRationale: Commercially funded port resilience work.
desc: Post-2016 Kaikōura earthquake strengthening of CentrePort Wellington wharves and facilities. Insurance and commercially funded. Critical for Cook Strait ferry operations and Wellington cargo.

name: Renewable Energy — Wind Farms (National)
riskRationale: Market-driven renewable energy investment. Commercially funded. Fast RMA consent pathway exists.
desc: Multiple wind farm projects across NZ: WestWind (Wellington, operational), Turitea (Manawatū, 222MW operational), Harapaki (Hawke's Bay, 176MW under construction), Kaiwera Downs (Southland, consented), Sandy Point (Southland, consented). Total ~1.5GW pipeline.

name: Three Kings Quarry Development — Auckland
riskRationale: Resource consent process; depends on Auckland Council and Kāinga Ora involvement.
desc: Development of the Three Kings quarry site for mixed housing (2,800+ homes). Resource consent granted after long battle. Requires substantial earthworks to level the site. Mixed social and market housing.

name: Ōtautahi Christchurch Convention Centre — Te Pae
riskRationale: Operating, nearing full use.
desc: Te Pae Christchurch Convention Centre — opened 2021, still completing fit-out. 2,500-seat auditorium, 4,000sqm of meeting space. Post-earthquake anchor project. Fully operational.

name: Marsden Point Import Terminal — Oil Refinery Conversion
riskRationale: Commercially funded, operationally critical for NZ fuel supply.
desc: Conversion of the Marsden Point oil refinery (closed 2022) to a fuel import terminal. New tankage, marine offloading, and pipeline infrastructure. NZ now imports all refined fuel. Commercially funded by Channel Infrastructure.

name: Greater Christchurch — Housing Programme
riskRationale: Kāinga Ora programme cuts affect Christchurch housing development.
desc: Kāinga Ora's Christchurch programme including post-earthquake urban renewal and new social housing. Areas include Greys Ave (Christchurch CBD), Bryndwr, and Linwood. Programme affected by National's 2024 Kāinga Ora budget cuts.

name: Dunedin Performing Arts — Redevelopment
riskRationale: Council-funded, local project.
desc: Redevelopment of the Dunedin performing arts precinct including refurbishment of His Majesty's Theatre and new black-box studio. Council capital project.

name: Northland Broadband and Digital Infrastructure
riskRationale: Crown-funded rural broadband — bipartisan.
desc: Extension of Ultra-Fast Broadband and Rural Broadband Initiative to remaining Northland communities. Includes fixed wireless and fibre to under-served rural areas. Crown Infrastructure Partners funded programme.
